cuisine: Burmese / Southeast Asian (served in Singapore context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese tomato salad; 'salad food product' (FOODON_00002539) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tomato-based products; used as closest defensible category for this tomato-dominant salad dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Burmese tomato salad; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side-dish serving of tomato salad is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as served in a hawker or casual dining context.

nutrient_source.title: M11 Tomato Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for M11 Tomato Salad (cr_id G00010951). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat composition typical of peanut-and-sesame-oil dressings; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese tomato salad. GI estimated at 38 based on the GI of raw tomatoes (GI ~38, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant ingredient. The high fat content (44.5 g/100g) from peanuts, sesame, and oil further suppresses glycemic response. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (38 × 5.9 available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.2, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; fish sauce and dried shrimp are primary contributors; potassium at only 64 mg/100 g is insufficient to offset sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request omission of dried shrimp and substitution of fish sauce with a low-sodium alternative, and restrict portion to no more than 50 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Dietary fibre is 0 g/100 g and total fat is 44.5 g/100 g; the very high fat content from peanuts, sesame, fried garlic, and oil increases caloric density and may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 50–75 g and request reduced oil and fried garlic to lower fat density; the low glycemic load (GL 2.2) is favourable but the high fat and sodium content require caution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 44.5 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~5.5 g/100 g, cholesterol 26 mg/100 g, and sodium 810 mg/100 g collectively present a high cardiovascular risk profile; fried garlic contributes advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to its high fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium content; if consumed occasionally, portion should be restricted to 50 g with reduced oil and no dried shrimp.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomatoes and chilli are nightshade vegetables containing solanine that may trigger joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; sodium 810 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~5.5 g/100 g may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid this dish; others should limit portion to 50 g, request reduced fish sauce and dried shrimp, and avoid fried garlic to minimise AGE exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Salad (ခရမ်းချဉ်သီးသုပ်, Kha-myin-chin-thi-thoke) is a Burmese-style dressed salad widely served in Singapore's hawker and casual dining settings. The dish centres on fresh or lightly blanched tomatoes tossed with roasted peanuts, toasted sesame seeds, fried garlic, dried shrimp, sliced onion, chilli, fish sauce, and cooking oil. The result is a richly flavoured, oily salad with a savoury-umami profile, mild heat, and a contrast of soft tomato flesh against crunchy peanuts and sesame. Despite its vegetable base, the dish is nutritionally dense: per 100 g it delivers 430 kcal, 44.5 g fat, 1.32 g protein, 5.9 g carbohydrate, and 810 mg sodium. Dietary fibre is reported as 0 g/100 g in the HPB source. The extremely high fat content derives primarily from peanuts, sesame, and added oil. The sodium level of 810 mg/100 g is clinically significant and is driven by fish sauce and dried shrimp. Cholesterol is 26 mg/100 g. The glycemic load is low (GL ~2.2 per 100 g serving) due to minimal available carbohydrate and fat-mediated suppression of glycemic response. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management due to its high sodium and fat load, and requires portion control for Type 2 Diabetes. Tomatoes and chilli are nightshade vegetables, making this dish a concern for rheumatoid arthritis-sensitive individuals. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains peanut, sesame, shellfish, and fish allergens.

tomato: ခရမ်းချဉ်သီး

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010951, confidence 0.95 in source record). Saturated fat is estimated from typical peanut-sesame-oil fat composition and documented as an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on raw tomato GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source record and is plausible given the preparation method (dressing-heavy, low intact fibre). 2. saturated_fat_g (5.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the fatty acid profile of peanut oil and sesame oil as dominant fat sources; HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB Tomato Salad preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input record labels primary_cuisine as 'Western', which is inconsistent with the dish's Burmese origin and Burmese-language name; this has been corrected to 'Burmese / Southeast Asian'. 5. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on raw tomato (GI 38, University of Sydney GI Database); the high fat content further suppresses glycemic response. 6. Halal status is marked false due to the presence of dried shrimp and fish sauce, which may not be halal-certified in all preparations.
